revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ar:إزاناغي]][[az:İzanaqi]][[bcl:Izanagi]][[bn:ইজানাগি]][[bs:Izanagi]][[ca:Izanagi]][[cs:Izanagi a Izanami]][[da:Izanagi]][[el:Ιζανάγκι]][[en:Izanagi]][[eo:Izanagi]][[es:Izanagi]][[fa:ایزاناگی]][[fi:Izanaki]][[fr:Izanagi]][[he:איזאנגי]][[hr:Izanagi]][[hu:Izanagi]][[id:Izanagi]][[it:Izanagi]][[ja:イザナギ]][[ka:იძანაგი]][[ko:이자나기]][[lt:Idzanagis]][[mk:Изанаги]][[mni:ꯏꯓꯥꯅꯥꯒꯤ]][[no:Izanagi]][[or:ଇଜାନାଗି]][[pt:Izanagi]][[ro:Izanagi]][[ru:Идзанаги]][[simple:Izanagi]][[sk:Izanagi]][[sl:Izanagi]][[sr:Изанаки]][[sv:Izanagi]][[ta:இசநாகி]][[th:อิซานางิ]][[tl:Izanagi]][[tr:İzanagi]][[uk:Ідзанаґі]][[vi:Izanagi]][[zh:伊邪那岐]] {{Short description|Deity of Shinto religion}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Izanagi-no-Mikoto | image =Kobayashi Izanami and Izanagi.jpg; File:Kobayashi Izanami and Izanagi.jpg| caption = {{Nihongo|''Searching the Seas with the [[Ame-no-Nuboko|Tenkei]]''|天瓊を以て滄海を探るの図|Tenkei o motte sōkai o saguru no zu}}. Painting by {{ill|Kobayashi Eitaku|en|Kobayashi Eitaku|qid=Q2784105|lt=Kobayashi Eitaku|1=Kobayashi Eitaku}}, 1880-90 (MFA, Boston). Izanagi with the spear [[Ame-no-Nuboko|Ame-no-Nuhoko]] (''Tenkei'') to the right, {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} to the left. | god_of = Primordial god of creation and life | script_name = Japanese | script = 伊邪那岐命, 伊耶那岐命, 伊弉諾尊 | other_names = Izanagi-no-Kami (伊邪那岐神)
Izanagi-no-Ōkami (伊邪那岐大神)
Kumano-Hayatama-no-Ōkami (熊野速玉大神) | cult_center = [[Taga-taisha|Taga Taisha]], [[Izanagi Jingu|Izanagi Shrine]], [[Eda Shrine]], [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], [[:ja:自凝島神社|Onokorojima Shrine]], [[:ja:三峯神社|Mitsumine Shrine]] and others | symbols = | consort ={{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}; {{ill|Izanami|qid=Q682306|1=Izanami}}| parents =None ({{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'')
Aokashikine-no-Mikoto (''Shoki'')
Awanagi-no-Mikoto (''Shoki'')
Omodaru and Ayakashikone{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Classic Texts : Omodaru, Ayakashikone|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210411072506/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=124|url-status=dead|archive-date=2021-04-11|access-date=2021-01-17|website=eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}; {{ill|Omodaru|qid=Q84864895|1=Omodaru}}; {{ill|Ayakashikone|qid=Q84864913|1=Ayakashikone}}| siblings = {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} | children = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
{{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Hiruko|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}
[[Kagu-tsuchi]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]'' | region = Japan | gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | offspring = {{ill|Ahashima|qid=Q13275856|1=Ahashima}}; {{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|1=Amaterasu}}; {{ill|Ebisu|qid=Q1129330|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}; {{ill|Hayaakitsuhiko and Hayaakitsuhime|qid=Q11326733|1=Hayakitsuhiko and Hayakitsuhime}}; {{ill|Hiruko|qid=Q1067028|1=Hiruko}}; {{ill|Idzunome|qid=Q10885126|1=Izunome}}; {{ill|Kagu-tsuchi|qid=Q1640658|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}; {{ill|Katakurokushin|qid=Q11455406|1=Katakurokushin}}; {{ill|Kaya-no-hime|qid=Q8191878|1=Kaya-no-hime}}; {{ill|Kukunochi|qid=Q10749920|1=Kukunochi}}; {{ill|Magatsuhinokami|qid=Q11591203|1=Magatsuhi-no-kami}}; {{ill|Nakisawame|qid=Q11144612|1=Nakisawame}}; {{ill|Naobinokami|qid=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}; {{ill|Oogotooshionokami|qid=Q10933447|1=Oogotooshionokami}}; {{ill|Shinatsuhiko|qid=Q1073925|1=Shinatsuhiko}}; {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|qid=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}; {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Tori no Iwakusufune no Kami|qid=Q10731425|1=Ame-no-torifune}}; {{ill|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q595520|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Watatsumi|qid=Q1474187|1=Watatsumi}}; {{ill|Ōgetsuhime|qid=Q3261482|1=Ōgetsuhime}}; {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|qid=Q386563|1=Ōyamatsumi}} | member_of = {{ill|Izanagi and Izanami|qid=Q12259961|1=Izanagi and Izanami}} | equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|qid=Q854773|1=Bhaisajyaguru}} }} '''Izanagi''' (イザナギ/伊邪那岐/伊弉諾) or '''Izanaki''' (イザナキ), formally referred to with a divine honorific as {{Nihongo|'''Izanagi-no-Mikoto'''|伊邪那岐命/伊弉諾尊|extra=meaning "He-who-invites" or the "Male-who-invites"}}, is the {{ill|Creator deity|en|Creator deity|qid=Q635155|lt=Creator deity|1=Creator deity}} (''[[kami]]'') of both creation and life in [[Japanese mythology]]. He and his sister-wife {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} are the last of the [[Kamiyonanayo|seven generations of primordial deities]] that manifested after the formation of heaven and earth. Izanagi and Izanami are held to be the creators of the [[Japanese archipelago]] and the progenitors of many deities, which include the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], the moon deity [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]], and the storm god [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]. He is a god that can be said to be the beginning of the current Japanese imperial family. ==Name== His name is given in the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} ({{Circa|712 AD}}) both as ''Izanagi-no-Kami'' (伊邪那岐神) and ''Izanagi-no-Mikoto'' (伊邪那岐命), while the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' (720 AD) refers to him as ''Izanagi-no-Mikoto'', with the name written in different {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=characters|1=Kanji}} (伊弉諾尊). The names ''Izanagi'' (''Izanaki'') and ''Izanami'' are often interpreted as being derived from the verb {{transliteration|ja|izanau}} ({{ill|Historical kana orthography|en|Historical kana orthography|qid=Q1142552|lt=historical orthography|1=Historical kana orthography}} {{transliteration|ja|izanafu}}) or ''iⁿzanap''- from Western Old Japanese 'to invite', with ''-ki'' / ''-gi'' and ''-mi'' being taken as masculine and feminine suffixes, respectively.{{cite web |last1=Kadoya |first1=Atsushi |title=Izanagi |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9385 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-03-23 }}Phillipi, Donald L. (1969). ''Kojiki''. Tokyo: University of Tokyo Press. p. 482.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj009.htm Section II.—The Seven Divine Generations.] The literal translation of Iⁿzanaŋgî and Iⁿzanamî are 'Male-who-invites' and 'Female-who-invites'.{{Cite book|last=Philippi|first=Donald L.|title=Kojiki|year=1968|pages=480}}{{Cite book|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|title=The Kojiki|year=1919|pages=19}} {{ill|Shiratori Kurakichi|en|Shiratori Kurakichi|qid=Q1188365|lt=Shiratori Kurakichi|1=Shiratori Kurakichi}} proposed an alternative theory which instead sees the root ''iza-'' (or rather ''isa-'') to be derived from ''isao'' (historical orthography: ''isawo'') meaning 'achievement' or 'merit'.{{cite book |last1=Matsumura |first1=Takeo |title=日本神話の研究, 第2巻 (Nihon shinwa no kenkyu, vol. 2) |date=1955 |publisher=Baifūkan |page=56}} The etymological origin of the verb is suggested to be a precursor to the Middle Korean lemma ''yènc''- meaning 'to place/put on [the top of]' reconstructed as ''*yenc-a'' (place-{{ill|Infinitive|en|Infinitive|qid=Q179230|lt=INF|1=Infinitive}}) in {{ill|Old Korean|en|Old Korean|qid=Q715364|lt=Old Korean|1=Old Korean}}.Vovin, Alexander. ''Immigrants or Overlords? Korean Influences on Japan in the Archaic Period: a Linguistic Perspective.'' Institut für Kultur und Geitestesgeschichte Asiens, June 14 2012. ==Mythology== [[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|thumb|left|170px|The first group of primordial deities, the ''[[kotoamatsukami]]'', and the seven generations of ''kami'' (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') that emerged after them]] ===In the ''Kojiki''=== ====The birth of the land==== {{See also|Izanami|Kuniumi|Kamiumi}} The {{lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}} portrays Izanagi and his younger twin sister Izanami as the [[Kamiyonanayo|seventh and final generation of deities]] that manifested after the emergence of the first group of gods, the [[Kotoamatsukami]], when heaven and Earth came into existence.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj009.htm Section II.—The Seven Divine Generations]. Receiving a command from the other gods to solidify and shape the Earth (which then "[resembled] floating oil and [drifted] like a jellyfish"), the couple use a [[Ame-no-Nuboko|jewelled spear]] to churn the watery chaos. The brine that dripped from the tip of the spear congealed and turned into an island named [[Onogoro Island|Onogoro]] (淤能碁呂島).Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj010.htm Section III.—The Island of Onogoro]. The two descended to the island and, setting up their dwelling, erected a 'heavenly pillar' (''ama no mihashira'') on it. Izanagi and Izanami, realizing that they were meant to procreate and have children, then devised a marriage ceremony whereby they would walk in opposite directions around the pillar, greet each other and initiate intercourse. After Izanami greeted Izanagi first, Izanagi objected that he, the man, should have been the first to speak. True enough, the first offspring that resulted from their union, the 'leech-child' {{ill|Ebisu (mythology)|en|Ebisu (mythology)|qid=Q1129330|lt=Ebisu (mythology)|1=Ebisu (mythology)}}, was considered imperfect and set adrift on a {{ill|Reed boat|en|Reed boat|qid=Q373613|lt=boat of reeds|1=Reed boat}}. Izanagi and Izanami then also begat the island of [[Awa (island)|Awa]] (淡島 ''Awashima''), but this too was not counted among their rightful progeny.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj011.htm Section IV.—Courtship of the Deities The Male-Who-Invites and the Female Who-Invites]. [[File:Japan yashima.png|thumb|The 'Eight Great Islands' (大八洲 ''Ōyashima'') of Japan begotten by Izanami and Izanagi]] Izanagi and Izanami then decided to repeat the ritual, with Izanagi greeting Izanami first. This time, their union was a success, with Izanami giving birth to some of the various islands that comprise the Japanese archipelago (with the notable exceptions of Shikoku and Hokkaido), which include the following eight islands (in the following order): *{{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|qid=Q504373|lt=Awaji-no-Ho-no-Sawake|1=Awaji Island}} (淡道之穂之狭別島) *The double-named island of Iyo (伊予之二名島 ''Iyo-no-Futana-no-Shima'', modern [[Shikoku]]) *The triple islands of {{ill|Oki Islands|en|Oki Islands|qid=Q483146|lt=Oki|1=Oki Islands}} (隠伎之三子島 ''Oki-no-Mitsugo-no-Shima'') *Tsukushi (筑紫島, modern [[Kyushu]]) *[[Iki Island|Iki]] (伊伎島) *[[Tsushima Island|Tsushima]] (津島) *[[Sado Island|Sado]] (佐度島) *Ōyamato-Toyoakitsushima (大倭豊秋津島, modern [[Honshu]])Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj012.htm Section V.—Birth of the Eight Islands]. The two then proceeded to beget the various deities who are to inhabit these lands.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj013.htm Section VI.—Birth of the Various Deities]. Izanami, however, was badly injured and eventually died after giving birth to the fire god [[Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi]].Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj014.htm Section VII.—Retirement of Her Augustness The Princess-Who-Invites]. In an act of grief and rage, Izanagi killed Kagutsuchi with his '[[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi|ten-grasp sword]]'. More gods manifest into existence out of Izanami's excreta, Kagutsuchi's blood and mutilated remains, and Izanagi's tears.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj015.htm Section VIII.—The Slaying of the Fire-Deity].Phillipi (1969). pp. 48–60. ====Descent into Yomi==== Izanagi, wishing to see Izanami again, went down to {{ill|Yomi|en|Yomi|qid=Q1142558|lt=Yomi|1=Yomi}}, the land of the dead, in the hopes of retrieving her. Izanami reveals that she had already partaken of food cooked in the furnace of the underworld, rendering her return impossible. Izanagi, losing his patience, betrayed his promise not to look at her and lit up a fire, only to find that Izanami is now a rotting corpse. To avenge her shame, Izanami dispatched the gods of thunder (known as the [[Yakusanoikazuchi]]), the "hags of Yomi" (予母都志許売 ''{{ill|Shikome|en|Shikome|qid=Q2390449|lt=Yomotsu-shikome|1=Shikome}}''), and a horde of warriors to chase after him. To distract them, Izanagi threw the vine securing his hair and the comb on his right hair-knot, which turned into [[:en:Grapes|Grapes]] and {{ill|Bamboo shoot|en|Bamboo shoot|qid=Q2157176|lt=Bamboo shoot|1=Bamboo shoot}}s that the hags devoured. Upon reaching the pass of {{ill|Yomotsu Hirasaka|en|Yomotsu Hirasaka|qid=Q22084535|lt=Yomotsu Hirasaka|1=Yomotsu Hirasaka}} (黄泉比良坂, the 'Flat Slope of Yomi'), Izanagi took three {{ill|Peach|en|Peach|qid=Q13189|lt=Peach|1=Peach}}es from a nearby tree and repelled his pursuers using them. He then declared the peach fruit to be divine and bade it to grow in the land of the living to help people in need. When Izanami herself came in pursuit of him, Izanagi sealed the entrance to Yomi using a huge boulder. Izanami then pronounces a curse, vowing to kill a thousand people each day, to which Izanagi replies that he will then beget a thousand and five hundred people everyday to thwart her.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj016.htm Section IX.—The Land of Hades].Phillipi (1969). pp. 61–67.{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=175|title=Shinto Portal - IJCC, Kokugakuin University}} ====Purification (''Misogi'')==== [[File:阿波岐原 - Awagihara.jpg|thumb|160px|Izanagi purifying himself (''[[misogi]]'') by immersing in a river ({{ill|Natori Shunsen|en|Natori Shunsen|qid=Q2344286|lt=Natori Shunsen|1=Natori Shunsen}})]] {{Further|Kamiumi#Purification of Izanagi}} Izanagi, feeling contaminated by his visit to Yomi, went to "[the plain of] Awagihara (i.e. a plain covered with ''{{ill|en:Lespedeza bicolor|en|en:Lespedeza bicolor|qid=Q232652|lt=awagi|1=Lespedeza bicolor}}'') by the river-mouth of Tachibana in {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|qid=Q513737|lt=Himuka|1=Hyūga Province}} in [the island of] [[Kyushu|Tsukushi]]" and [[Misogi|purified himself]] by bathing in the river; various deities came into existence as he stripped off his clothes and accouterments and immersed himself in the water. The three most important ''kami'', the "Three Precious Children" (三貴子 {{transliteration|ja|mihashira no uzu no miko}} or {{transliteration|ja|sankishi}}) – the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], the moon deity [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]], and the storm god [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] – were born when Izanagi washed his left eye, his right eye, and his nose, respectively.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj017.htm Section X.—The Purification of the August Person].Phillipi (1969). pp. 68–70. ====Izanagi and Susanoo==== {{see also|Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Amaterasu}} Izanagi divides the world among his three children: Amaterasu was allotted [[Takamagahara]] (高天原, the "Plain of High Heaven"), Tsukuyomi the night, and Susanoo the seas.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj018.htm Section XI.—Investiture of the Three Deities; The Illustrious August Children]. Susanoo did not perform his appointed task and instead kept crying and howling "until his beard eight hands long extended down over his chest," causing the mountains to wither and the rivers to dry up. After saying to his father that he wished to go to his mother's land, {{ill|Ne-no-kuni|en|Ne-no-kuni|qid=Q7555425|lt=Ne-no-Katasu-Kuni|1=Ne-no-kuni}} (根堅州国, the 'Land of Roots'), a furious Izanagi then expelled Susanoo "with a divine expulsion," after which he disappears from the narrative.Chamberlain (1882). [https://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj019.htm Section XII.—The Crying and Weeping of His Impetuous-Male-Augustness].Phillipi (1969). pp. 71–73. ===In the ''Nihon Shoki''=== While the first generations of ''kami'' including Izanagi and Izanami are implied in the {{lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}} and the {{lang|ja-latn|Nihon Shoki}}'s main narrative to have manifested independent of each other, one variant cited in the ''Shoki'' instead describes them as the offspring of Aokashikine-no-Mikoto (青橿城根尊), another name for the goddess Ayakashikone-no-Mikoto, of the sixth of the first seven generations of gods. Another variant meanwhile portrays Izanagi as the offspring of a deity named Awanagi-no-Mikoto (沫蕩尊) and the fifth-generation descendant of the primordial deity [[Kuni-no-Tokotachi|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto.]]{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=7}} In the ''Shoki'''s main narrative, the couple first begets the following eight islands after performing the marriage ceremony (in the following order): *Awaji (淡路洲), which "was reckoned as the {{ill|Placenta|en|Placenta|qid=Q1212935|lt=Placenta|1=Placenta}}, and their minds took no pleasure in it" *Ōyamato-Toyoakitsushima (大日本豊秋津洲) *The double-named island of Iyo (伊豫二名洲) *Tsukushi (筑紫洲) *Oki (億岐洲) and Sado (佐度洲), born as twins *{{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|qid=Q1351611|lt=Koshi|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} (越洲), what is now known as the {{ill|Hokuriku region|en|Hokuriku region|qid=Q381025|lt=Hokuriku region|1=Hokuriku region}} *Ōshima (大洲), identified with the island of {{ill|Suō-Ōshima|en|Suō-Ōshima|qid=Q1205034|lt=Suō-Ōshima|1=Suō-Ōshima}} in {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}} *Kibi-no-Kojima (吉備子洲), identified with the [[:ja:児島半島|Kojima Peninsula]] in southern {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}} (formerly the {{ill|Kibi Province|en|Kibi Province|qid=Q1155128|lt=province of Kibi|1=Kingdom of Kibi}}) Both Ōshima and Kibi-no-Kojima are not reckoned among the eight great islands in the {{lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}, instead being identified as being born after them. The other remaining islands, such as Tsushima (Tsushima Province ) and Iki (壱岐島), are said to have been produced by the coagulation of the {{ill|Sea foam|en|Sea foam|qid=Q249020|lt=foam in sea water|1=Sea foam}} (or freshwater).{{cite wikisource |author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspage=13}} ==Genealogy== {{Seven Generations of Kami}} ==See also== * {{ill|Twins in mythology|en|Twins in mythology|qid=Q4088159|lt=Twins in mythology|1=Twins in mythology}} *[[Shinto in popular culture]] * [[:en:Izanagi Plate|Izanagi Plate]] * {{ill|Pangu|en|Pangu|qid=Q245395|lt=Pangu|1=Pangu}} – Chinese creator god, who also created the sun and the moon from his eyes. * {{ill|Ishana|en|Ishana|qid=Q608366|lt=Ishana|1=Ishana}} * [[Ame-no-ohabari]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book |last1=Aston |first1=William George |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. |location=London |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697}} *{{cite book |last1=Chamberlain |first1=Basil H. |title=A Translation of the "Ko-Ji-Ki," or "Records of Ancient Matters" |date=1882 |publisher=Lane, Crawford & Co. |location=Yokohama |url=http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm}} *Philippi, Donald L., tr. (1968/1969). {{lang|ja-latn|Kojiki}}. University of Tokyo Press. {{ISBN|978-0691061603}}. ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Izanagi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/izanagi Japanese History Database] {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Shirayamahime Faith}} {{Izanagi Faith}} [[en:Izanagi]] [[en:Izanagi]] [[ar:إزاناغي]] [[bcl:Izanagi]] [[bn:ইজানাগি]] [[bs:Izanagi]] [[ca:Izanagi]] {{Commons category|Izanagi}} [[cs:Izanagi a Izanami]] [[da:Izanagi]] [[el:Ιζανάγκι]] [[eo:Izanagi]] [[es:Izanagi]] [[fa:ایزاناگی]] [[fi:Izanaki]] [[fr:Izanagi]] [[he:איזאנגי]] [[hr:Izanagi]] [[hu:Izanagi]] [[id:Izanagi]] [[it:Izanagi]] [[ja:イザナギ]] [[ka:იძანაგი]] [[ko:이자나기]] [[lt:Idzanagis]] [[mk:Изанаги]] [[mni:ꯏꯓꯥꯅꯥꯒꯤ]] [[no:Izanagi]] [[or:ଇଜାନାଗି]] [[pt:Izanagi]] [[ro:Izanagi]] [[ru:Идзанаги]] [[simple:Izanagi]] [[sk:Izanagi]] [[sl:Izanagi]] [[sr:Изанаки]] [[sv:Izanagi]] [[ta:இசநாகி]] [[th:อิซานางิ]] [[tl:Izanagi]] [[tr:İzanagi]] [[uk:Ідзанаґі]] [[vi:Izanagi]] [[zh:伊邪那岐]] {{wikidata link|Q813858}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Creation myths]] [[Category:Creator gods]] [[Category:Divine twins]] [[Category:Health gods]] [[Category:Japanese gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 100+ untranslated japanese characters]]
